sentences.text: Yes.
I worked very hard, physically, there.
And lived very insecurely all the time, because the danger was there that I'll meet somebody, that he will recognize me.
The danger was that I will not be able-- somebody will discover we shared four in a room in the dormitory.
There were all kinds of things.
I was completely cut off from the world outside.
I didn't know what was going on.
The newspapers, the radio, didn't notify what was going on.
I-- I-- I just thought, I'm the only Jewish person who survived, in a way.
You know, completely isolated.
Because I was afraid of Ukrainian-- because there were circles.
There were Italian, all kinds of people working there-- I sort of joined a Polish group of people.
We-- on my day off, I would meet them.
And, of course, Polish people are very patriotic and also anti-Semitic, and I didn't trust them.
But there was a young boy there who I trusted, and very stupidly.
I mean, it was so stupid to-- to-- but I had the need to tell somebody, because I told him-- one of the things I told him, if something happens to me, try to find other family.
Because I was hoping somebody from the family somehow will survive, and you'll notify them.
He behaved like a gentleman.
He was correct in his behavior.
He didn't use this knowledge as-- as a weapon or to-- to-- to make me live with him or whatever.
He was really a-- a-- a very nice young man who was supposed to be in love with me.
Except that it was a outlet.
You know, I could be, with him, myself for a little while.

Not when we were in a group, but when we were on our own, I could-- this was a sort of wonderful support, in a way, that for one minute in a week, you know, or one five minutes, I could go back and to be myself.
And this was a great help at that time.
I helped him in a way, because he was involved with a German woman.
And this was dangerous, because three of the Polish boys were hanged.
And you didn't have-- you-- you were not supposed to mix the races, you know.
And therefore it was-- the danger of that.
And he had a girlfriend, so all the time with him I was talking about his girlfriend, trying to-- to encourage him that he should rather leave this German woman and be with his girlfriend.
So in a way, it got convenient-- it was convenient for me to encourage this relationship with his girlfriend.
But it was a comfort, because for a little while I could be myself.
But all the stages, I have never believed that I will survive.
I always-- not one-- even-- which was [INAUDIBLE].
Somehow, this-- this-- this-- my grandmother saying, 'there's oil on top of the water,' I said, somehow, it must come.
The Germans, who are so methodical, so exact, so bureaucratic, it must come out somehow, somewhere, you know?
So all the time I have lived on the edge of life and death.
All the time.
It-- it-- it needed-- I mean, work was, in a way, good, because I occupied myself with the work I did.
But the tensions and the-- the-- the pressures and the fear-- this-- this-- the this is-- that-- that somehow it will come to an end.
And you were constantly thinking about it when you weren't doing something else?
Of course.
I mean, my relief was-- you see, unfortunately, when I was in Berlin, I had the room to myself.
When I was here, because we were six or more, I shared a bedroom with somebody.
So I didn't have even that privacy to be by myself.
I could go for a walk, perhaps, you know, and to-- to just be by myself.
I didn't think.
I just tried to push away any kind of thinking, because thinking would be unbearable.
And I didn't know.
I was hoping against hope, or kidding myself, that they are working somewhere in a camp, that my mother is in the ghetto in Jaworow.
You know, thinking all the time.
And all the time dreaming the coming together, this wonderful-- I always imagined a festival, the feast that we will make.
Because food was still also-- at that time, I wasn't starving, but the food was very important, that the feast of the family coming together.
This was something that I carried with me, that this will be this wonderful moment when we're all gathered together after the war.
This was the-- the strength you had to continue, that you felt that somehow, we will come together with all-- we will overcome this difficult period, and that we will be, after the war, together.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGH] It's-- it's-- it's difficult to believe that I could-- that I-- that I kid myself.
I don't know.
It was-- it was-- you couldn't believe in the atrocities.
You couldn't believe that it happened.
I mean, it was so-- nowadays we are more conditioned to it with the history from the past.
We know.
But if you look at '42, who could have imagined this mass production?
Very often, when I-- you know, I try now to read and to look.
There is a lot of literature coming on.
There is a lot.
I mean, one of the best documents of that period is Primo Levi, because he is really-- he became a writer through his experience.
Was being trained a scientist.
He's a witness, and he does it in a beautiful language and a very-- very simply representing bearing witness to that period.
And I think his writing is one of the best.
One of the best films of that period is which is a documentary.
Very often, my son tells me, why are you looking for it?
Why do you punish yourself?
Why do you go for that?
Because, I tell them, I'm also interested in that period.
You know, I know only one narrow, narrow point from my own experiences.
I don't know what happened on the right, on the left.
I didn't know anything which was happening around me.
To begin with, I used to look for pictures or photographs.
Maybe I'll recognize a face.
Maybe somebody will be there that I-- because people did sometimes recognize.
This hope has failed.

But I try to-- to-- to-- I'm talking about 30 years ago, immediately after the war-- looking at that period, learning just as much as anybody else, because I-- you know, every experience is-- is channeled into a very narrow path what they had to do-- to do.
Now I look at it differently.
I look at it how the new generation is representing this period.
How artistically it's represented.
Nothing represents this terrible fear, this anxiety, this helplessness.
This-- we were treated like vermin, and we were-- became to think like that, because there was no hope.
There was no-- nobody to protect you.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Nobody to-- you could go to for any justice or any fairness or any reprieve.
You were just there.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And anybody could do-- I'm talking before I was in Germany-- anybody could do with you whatever you wanted to.
And this is a terrible feeling of insecurity, which remains with you all the time.
Because you know how quickly all this can be destroyed or disturbed.
And it left an indelible mark.
I think all we survivors are damaged [BACKGROUND NOISE] beyond repair.

But at that stage, in-- in-- in-- in Germany, when I felt that Germany's beginning to lose, I was hoping, you know, that for the liberation, I was looking forward to this moment of liberating and going back to Poland and finding the family who are somewhere, you know, in different ways, perhaps survived.
But my liberation was another nightmare, because I was liberated by the Russians.
And the Russians have never taken any account, whether you were Jewish, or Russian, or Polish, or German.
They were raping and loitering all throughout.
And it was another nightmarish experience, which I personally took very badly, because being liberated-- I took it as a punishment that I survived, that I deserved that.
Yeah.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
[DRINKS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't know whether you want to talk about it, but it was a nightmarish experience, because it happened after I was liberated.
I didn't survive-- a lot of people survived, and I justify them completely, you know, because of different methods-- living with somebody-- this wasn't it.
I always remembered the [BACKGROUND NOISE] last words of my mother's.
When we said goodbye, she didn't tell me, I hope you survive, I hope you succeed.
She told me two things, and this remained my testament. 'Remember who you are, and tell them that there was nobody to talk to.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I try to remember who I am.
[DRINKS] I tried to behave in the way which would be proud of me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was-- when I look back, I understand much more than I understood then, because you can see the pattern.
You can see how it evolved.
You can see what was happening.
It happened because they were very, very clever.
They did it in a very perfidious, in a very methodical, in a very gradual way, and they did it in secrecy.
One of the things, they did it because it was conducive-- the environment.
Nobody cared.
Nobody bothered.
I remember when I was in Crossen an der Oder making some beds-- and this is when my Zionism started.
I didn't-- I wasn't political, I told you before.
But I felt, of course they can do with us what they want.
They can treat us like vermin, because we have got nobody protect us.
If we had a place to our own, somebody somewhere would be a voice who would say, look what is happening.
In my simple way, this is how-- this was the awakening, because I realized we were treated like vermin.
Nobody cared.
Nobody voiced a protest.
Nobody-- there was no contact.
Nobody.
And now I read the history of that period, I even feel worse.
But this is-- it is-- this is what happened.
And this is-- you know, in the worst of the moment of my survival, I always felt we are the sacrificial lamb for a better world to come.
But when I look around, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I don't see it.
It's very depressing that in our generation we are witnessing things which we thought will never happen again.
And this is why I feel what you are doing is important.
Not to tell my story.
This is not important.
I am just representing somebody from this generation, from this part of the world, who didn't choose to be born Jewish or didn't choose to be born Polish, but it was that.
And the price I had to pay for that.
And I feel that only education can help.
Only people-- if they are aware what is happening anywhere else.
And we also have to be aware of what is happening now anywhere else-- can build a better world to come.
Because man is capable of terrible things.
At the same time, man is capable of very noble things, but it depends which part of-- is developed which time.
And I think that, if we are aware-- because very often, if you know about it when it's too late, you can't do anything.
Nobody could have done very much, once you were in that situation.
But perhaps if we can see the signals and the symptoms to prevent it happening again, that perhaps our generation had some kind of a responsibility to do that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But, you know, I always had a difficult situation-- to talk, or not to talk.
To be, or not to be.
It was always with me, this conflict.
Because when you talk, first of all, you have to have a sympathetic listener to be able to talk.
Secondly, you don't want to talk about yourself.
I don't want any sympathy from anybody.
I was tough.
Life toughened me, and it happened so that I survived, not because I was clever.
Not because I was-- it's just a coincidence played a part.
You know, there wasn't a design in it.
It was just one step after another.
But I want to talk about the people who did not survive, people who were wiped out, who-- I don't even have a photograph of my sisters.
This is something that-- that I want people to be aware what-- the danger that can happen, because it is there all the time.
My survival-- these experiences and my liberation were very dramatic, and also left an indelible mark.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because it happened when I was liberated, because it all happened, so I took it as a-- as a-- as-- to justify my guilt of survival.
Because it's there all the time.
I feel guilty that I live.
And very often, when I'm very depressed and things are going very badly, I think, it's good for me to-- to-- to-- to bear the pain.
Because I think the few moments I could have been with my mother were more worthwhile than all my existence after that.
But I had to learn how to live with it.
And I thought I've done quite-- [LAUGHS] I'm sorry I'm not very good at your interview.
[LAUGHS] I thought that I'll be able to tell you this story bravely, because I couldn't have done this before.
But I thought I'd-- but yet it touches this inner nerve.
So I apologize for that.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's there all the time.
I live with it.
And my main preoccupation originally was to put it behind.
You know, my husband was also a survivor, but he escaped from Warsaw in 1939/40.
He had a fantastic story of survival.
And he could talk about it easier, because his was like adventure story.
You know, he went through Russia, to Japan, to Canada.
He came-- he was in the army.
I could never talk about it so much.
We decided-- because I would break down.
And very often-- we decided, at that stage, that we will put this behind us, that we want-- the purpose of having a family was to rebuild the family.
And the purpose of that was also that if we have a family, we give them an equal chance.
We give them-- that we don't want to bring them up under the shadow of our experiences, because very often the second generation suffers very much.
And if there is any achievement that I can comfort myself with my life, it is that I brought up two sons who were normal.
I don't consider anything else-- that they were normal human beings.
Because I've seen things which went very wrong, because automatically you are very possessive.
You concentrate all your love on your-- every mother does, but this is exaggerating.
You know, your insecurity, you hand over to the next generation.
Your fears, your insecurity, the way you handle babies.
Well, I've managed to do that reasonably well.
So I feel that at least my children-- I've never talked to my children properly about my experiences.
They were too young.
My grandchildren-- I talk to them more than to my children.
As a matter of fact, Martin, in his last visit when he came home-- by that time, he was a reporter.
And he had all his equipment, and he was going to interview me.
And he said mummy, I would like to tell you the story.
I mean, this was 10 years ago-- 12 years ago.
And we started, and I broke down.
So he said, well, never mind.
We'll do it another time.
Well, there wasn't another time, because he was killed soon after that.
So all the time, I felt that I had-- I wanted to talk about it not only as a historical document, but also to tell the family, in a way, in a continuous way.
So I hope-- but then, you see, I think that I haven't done it well, either.
It's difficult to find-- it's difficult to express in words [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the nightmarish years.
It wasn't just one.
This-- this-- this terrible fear, which was in us, this helplessness.
The-- the-- the cruelty, the humiliation.
It's unbearable.
It's very difficult to describe it in words.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's done.
And some of the writing is very good.
And I hope that-- that people will learn something from that period.
Well, I was, again, doubly broken down.
So I started walking back, you know?
I've-- I came back.
And the first Jewish community-- Jewish people I met was in Czestochowa.
You know Czestochowa is near Auschwitz, next to Auschwitz.
And there was already a Jewish community.
And I went over there, because I was in a terrible state.
And-- and-- so they gave me some money to support.
And there were two young men who interviewed the people as they were coming back.
And they felt that my documents were very interesting, because they were not false documents.
You know, they were really creative.
They were really created.
They were real documents all along.
So-- and I was so eager to go back to being Henia Meller, that I said, yes, you can have it.
Now I regret it.
I would have liked to have the documents.
And I've searched for them in Yad Vashem.
Somebody told me-- and I couldn't find it there-- that maybe there is in Warsaw a-- a document-- a institute for Jewish history, that maybe I'll find there.
Maybe one day I'll go to Poland.
Maybe one day I'll write to get it.
I would have liked to have the documents, just to prove to myself--
To those people, because they were collecting.
And I just wanted to go back.
I didn't want to have any part of it.
And I said, well, if it's useful for you, have it.
Now I wish I would have kept it.
So there I stayed overnight with a Jewish family.
And then I was trying to trace back, to go back to Sadowa Wisznia.
Obviously Sadowa Wisznia-- I didn't realize that the borderline, between Poland and Russia was going through Przemysl, which is next to Sadowa Wisznia, is in Poland.
Sadowa Wisznia and Lwow is in the Ukraine.
So I had to go-- to cross the border.
And having this experience with Russians, I didn't want to see another Russian in my life.
And I didn't want to go to Russia, because I was afraid that I won't come back.
But in Przemysl I met two or three people who survived in Sadowa Wisznia all through the war.
And they told me that there is nobody there, nobody.
I knew certain things which we had buried, and I didn't want to go for that.
So I stayed there a few days.
And, you know, we had a habit, when we were coming back, that we would sign in each Jewish community we passed, in these small towns or big towns, we would put a name.
There was a list.
And I put my name.
And then, when looking back, I realized that my mother's cousin, [?
Regina ?] [?
Wallach, ?] survived.
And she is now living in Lublin.
So I, from Przemysl, went to Lublin.
Lublin, at that time, was the capital of Poland.
But very soon, it moved to Warsaw.
And I met her.
And she was-- obviously, this was my first relative that I met.
And the reunion was very warm.
She shared a room with another woman, which was as big as a corridor, a little corridor.
But she gave me her bed, iron bed.
And I felt terrible having to stay this overnight there.
So very soon, I tried to-- to-- to get independent.
I managed to get a job.
I started working in the Jewish community half day to maintain myself.
And there were a lot of going on.
And I started studying.
My main concern was to go back to education.
You know, again, this emphasis on education.
And it had a double meaning.
It wasn't only-- but to restore my dignity, my-- myself, something.
So I started going half-day to school.
And then, in this little cell, she told me about another cousin, my mother's cousin, who survived Auschwitz.
And his name was [INAUDIBLE] [?
Wallach. ?] It was very unusual, because he was ill before the war already, had TB, which was very unusual for a Jewish boy at that time to have TB.
And he would be the last person, I would think, that he survived Auschwitz.
But he did.
And we three met together.
I sort of followed him.
I went to visit him.
He was in Katowice.
And we sort of had the three of us together.
Also, we lived in a different town, but we were my new family.
Well, it happened so that he had a sister who lived in-- in Paris.
She had a sister who lived in Israel-- who went to Israel from Vienna before the war.
So they all said, well, don't worry.
When we will be there, we'll take you.
So they left off, and I was then living in Warsaw-- working and studying.
And then Rabbi Schonfeld came from London.
I hope that next year we'll have a reunion, because it will be 50 years.
He was a magnificent man, larger than life.
I don't know why not enough respect and not enough is done in his-- for him in his name.
There must have been some community politics, which I don't know the details of.
But he was really a remarkable man.
He came-- he was an Orthodox rabbi, but he came in a-- to Warsaw in a uniform of an English captain.
And you know the Poles, when they see a English captain uniform, will fall overhead.
And he was very intelligent.
He had a list of 60 children who had family in London and wanted to bring the-- knew that the children survived.
So as he had experience of bringing children before the war from Vienna, he was sent to bring over the 60 children.
And I did work in the community, so I came in contact with him.
And he was very-- very intelligent.
Poland, at that time, was still flexible.
It wasn't rigid.
And he realized that he could-- instead of 60, he could bring out 120, with bribery or whatever it was.
So he gave me a fictitious family in London.
And he brought me.
I was one of the first-- in his first transport.
Later on, he brought two more [INAUDIBLE].
But he was a remarkable man.
I didn't know a word of English.
And I remember he was-- being a rabbi, I didn't realize the-- the-- the situation, that he was teaching us English song from 'Bicycle Built for Two.' And singing with us.
He was remarkable-- took individual interest, took individual-- was very involved.
And he was really a man who did something.
He didn't just talk about it.
He didn't sit on the committee.
He really went and did something.
And I owe him everything, because, for me, coming to London and to England was a new life.
In Poland, I would have been always left with a past.
To Palestine, which was really my aim to go, at that time was illegal immigration.
My cousin who went with the illegal immigration was stuck in Italy, and it was dangerous.
And I just wanted to get out of Poland as soon as I can.
At that time, to be able to come to London was like winning the lottery nowadays.
So I-- I came to England.
And we lived in a hostel.
The children who had families went to the families, and we stayed in a hostel.
And the poor man didn't know what to do with us.
In Stoke Newington.
The trouble was, you see, we all expected, you know, that we will have a palace-- car waiting for us, a Prince Charming.
And everybody wanted to study medicine.
You know, we had high expectations that the-- the Jewish community will-- people didn't know very much about us, didn't know how to treat us.
Nowadays, if you have some catastrophe, you've got counseling.
You have things.
At that time, people didn't know how to-- we looked normal, but we were not.
You know, we were all damaged in one way or another.
And we needed special treatment.
But we didn't-- we didn't-- we-- people didn't know enough about it to know how to handle us, how to treat us.
And the poor rabbi-- he had such a difficult time, because everybody pulled him in each way.
And I reminded myself that before the war, a cousin of my mother's came from London.
And I didn't know her name.
And I knew she wouldn't know my name.
But I was very keen to lighten of-- to take off the load of his shoulders, because he had got so many problems.
So I thought, if I can just remove myself-- he's done his job.
He brought me over.
If I can do something myself, it would be a help to him.
And so he gave an advert to the that the granddaughter of-- is looking for the daughter.
Because I only knew that.
And she responded to that advert and came to collect me.
It was good and bad, in a way, because she-- maybe she meant it well, but she was very tactless.
You know, she still hasn't outgrown-- she brought with her not the good quality, but the bad quality for the small shtetl.
She inherited not the good qualities of the eastern, but the bad qualities of-- and the mixture was very difficult.
And I-- I needed a different treatment.
I mean, she would-- wanted to know what happened to the family.
So if I told her, she said, yes, if somebody of my family would have survived-- and there was I, so clinging, wanting the family.
But to her, you know, I was so far removed that I wasn't family.
And then she would say-- not thinking about it, not because she was a bad woman, she was just tactless.
She would say, yes, good people didn't survive.
[YIDDISH].
It means, 'the rubbish survived.' But this is the reaction.
So very soon after that, I shut up.
I realized.
And this is how it is in life, somehow.
Something you do, and you get it back in one form or another.
When I was on the boat, because I was one of the oldest one, I wanted to help Rabbi Schonfeld.
You could see-- I have photographs where there were children of three and older.
So I was the one who emptied the sick bags, because we didn't have any nurses around, and who looked after the smaller children.
We were very lucky, because we got crates of fruit, which was a rarity.
I mean, in Poland, even before the war, the only time you had an orange, when you were dying.
But there we had, for the first time, some apples, some fruit.
And I was dividing them for the children.
And an elderly lady was standing in the corner, travelling very badly.
And I had some, so I gave it to her.
And she told me her story.
She came from Warsaw.
She was coming to her son, who was a lecturer in Oxford.
And through her-- and she gave me her address, because I didn't have my address.
And this is how sometimes it goes by.

When I was in the hostel, looking-- and also later on, when I wasn't in the hostel-- a way out of that situation, what am I going to do with my life, the family decided that I should go to a factory and work as a machinist.
I had some other inspiration.
I wanted to go back to education somehow.
I reminded myself about this woman, and she-- and he was in charge, in Poland.
At that time, there was a Polish university course.
And the Polish-- he was in charge of that.
And he gave me the address of this Polish university course.
